text:
HEMATOLOGY/MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
OFFICE VISIT NOTE -- TREATMENT ASSESSMENT

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
ATTENDING: Hematology/Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, treatment review

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old male with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS, BRAF wild-type) presenting today for interim response assessment and toxicity review following 4 cycles of mFOLFOX-6 plus Avastin.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski was diagnosed approximately 2 months ago with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous liver metastases. At the time of diagnosis, staging CT demonstrated a 4.1 cm sigmoid primary with pericolonic adenopathy and four bilobar hepatic lesions (largest 3.2 cm, right lobe Segment VI), CEA 148.2 ng/mL, CA 19-9 312 U/mL. Comprehensive genomic profiling confirmed KRAS G12D oncogenic driver, TP53 R248W, APC E1554* truncating mutation, and SMAD4 R361H, MSS/pMMR. Given bilobar but potentially convertible liver disease and absence of extrahepatic spread, multidisciplinary tumor board recommended conversion-intent chemotherapy with mFOLFOX-6 plus Avastin with the goal of reassessing hepatic resectability after 6-8 cycles.

He has now completed 4 cycles. Today's visit is prompted by results of repeat cross-sectional imaging performed after cycle 4, which I reviewed in detail ahead of this appointment.

IMAGING RESULTS (today's review): CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast performed approximately 1 week ago demonstrates a favorable response. The sigmoid primary has decreased from 4.1 x 3.8 cm to 3.6 x 3.2 cm. The pericolonic lymph nodes are significantly reduced, with the largest now 0.9 cm vs. 1.5 cm at baseline. All four hepatic lesions are smaller: the dominant Segment VI lesion decreased from 3.2 x 2.8 cm to 2.6 x 2.2 cm; Segment IVb from 1.8 x 1.6 cm to 1.3 x 1.2 cm; Segment II from 1.4 x 1.1 cm to 1.0 x 0.9 cm; and Segment VII from 0.9 x 0.8 cm to 0.7 x 0.6 cm. No new disease sites. No pulmonary nodules. This represents a meaningful reduction in hepatic disease burden across all lesions -- roughly 28% decrease in tumor measurement sum -- consistent with a partial response. No evidence of new extrahepatic spread.

TUMOR MARKERS: CEA 67.4 ng/mL today, down substantially from 148.2 at baseline -- roughly 55% decline. CA 19-9 was not repeated this cycle but will be re-checked today.

TOXICITY REVIEW:
- Peripheral neuropathy: Patient began reporting fingertip tingling and cold dysesthesias after cycle 3, consistent with acute Eloxatin neurotoxicity. Currently Grade 1 by CTCAE -- bilateral fingertip numbness and tingling, predominantly cold-triggered, does not interfere with daily function. He is using gloves outdoors and avoiding handling cold items, with adequate symptom management.
- Hypertension: Noted elevated blood pressure at prior visit (148/92 mmHg), attributed to bev. Lisinopril was increased from 10 mg to 20 mg daily at that time. Blood pressure today reviewed below.
- Nausea/fatigue: Grade 1 nausea, well-controlled with ondansetron as prescribed. Grade 1 fatigue -- describes mild reduced energy but able to perform all activities of daily living, walking, light housework.
- No mucositis, hand-foot syndrome, or significant hematologic toxicity reported.
- No thrombotic events, wound complications, or hemoptysis (bev-associated concerns).

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Mild fatigue as above. No fevers, no night sweats, no significant weight loss (weight today stable within 1 lb of cycle 1 weight). No rigors.
GI: No significant nausea at rest, mild with day of infusion managed with antiemetics. No vomiting. No diarrhea, constipation, or rectal bleeding. Bowel function normal. No abdominal pain. No change in stool caliber.
Neurologic: Bilateral fingertip tingling and numbness as described. Cold dysesthesias. No difficulty walking, no gait instability, no weakness.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no shortness of breath.
Respiratory: No cough, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain or arthralgias.
Dermatologic: No skin rash, no hand-foot syndrome.
Other: No new complaints.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 136/84 mmHg (left arm, seated), heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16, temperature 98.4 F, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 175 lbs (BMI 27.1).
General: Well-appearing male, in no acute distress, alert and oriented, pleasant and engaged.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. No mucositis.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Chest: Right chest wall port-a-cath site without erythema, tenderness, or signs of infection. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, nontender, nondistended. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No peritoneal signs. No palpable abdominal masses.
Extremities: No edema. No calf tenderness.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. Upper extremity: intact grip strength bilaterally. Decreased light touch sensation fingertips bilaterally (Grade 1). Lower extremity: Normal strength. Vibratory sense intact in great toes. Gait normal.
Skin: No rash, no palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (today)
CBC: WBC 5.8 K/uL (ANC 3.2), Hgb 11.2 g/dL (mild anemia, stable), Hct 34.1%, platelets 228 K/uL.
CMP: Na 139, K 4.1, Cl 101, CO2 25, BUN 16, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60). Glucose 112. ALT 58, AST 54 (mildly elevated, improved from prior visit ALT 68, AST 62; likely reflecting hepatic metastatic burden, now slightly improved consistent with imaging response). Total bilirubin 0.8, alkaline phosphatase 112 (mildly elevated). Albumin 3.9.
Urine protein:creatinine ratio: 0.16 (within acceptable range; Avastin monitoring, <2.0).
CEA: 67.4 ng/mL (significantly down from 148.2 at baseline).
CA 19-9: Pending (ordered today).
INR: 1.0.
Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (low-normal; will supplement given oxali use).

---

IMAGING
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (recent): As detailed above in HPI -- partial response with decrease in hepatic lesions across all four sites and decrease in sigmoid primary. No new lesions. No peritoneal disease. No ascites.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS), with synchronous liver metastases -- PARTIAL RESPONSE to mFOLFOX-6 plus Avastin after 4 cycles.

The disease is responding to treatment. All four hepatic lesions have shrunk, the sigmoid primary is smaller, and the pericolonic nodes have largely resolved. Tumor markers are tracking in the right direction with CEA dropping by more than half from baseline. This is an encouraging early response. The liver disease remains bilobar, and although the trajectory is favorable, it is too early to determine whether conversion to a fully resectable state is achievable. Per our initial plan, we will complete a total of 8 cycles and then perform formal reassessment with repeat hepatobiliary surgery tumor board review.

Plan:
- Continue mFOLFOX-6 plus bev. Today we will proceed with Cycle 5 Day 1 pending normal vital signs and acceptable labs (reviewed above -- appropriate to proceed).
- oxali dose maintained at full dose (85 mg/m2) given Grade 1 neuropathy only at this time. Will reduce by 25% if neuropathy progresses to Grade 2 or higher.
- Repeat imaging after Cycle 8 with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (full chest to be included given planned surgical reassessment).
- Formal hepatobiliary surgery tumor board re-presentation planned after Cycle 8 imaging.
- CEA and CA 19-9 to be checked with each cycle.

2. Grade 1 Eloxatin-induced peripheral neuropathy.
Currently manageable and does not require dose modification. Patient counseled to continue cold avoidance strategies (gloves, avoiding cold beverages). Symptom diary encouraged. If numbness/tingling progresses to persistent at-rest symptoms affecting fine motor function (i.e., Grade 2), will reduce oxali by 25%. If further progression to Grade 3, Eloxatin will be discontinued.

3. bev-associated hypertension -- improving.
Blood pressure today 136/84 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg (dose increased at prior visit from 10 mg). This is acceptable control (target <140/90 mmHg). Will continue lisinopril 20 mg. Home blood pressure monitoring encouraged, patient has a cuff at home. Will reassess at next visit. Proteinuria monitoring ongoing; UPC ratio 0.16 today, acceptable.

4. Mild anemia (Hgb 11.2).
Likely combination of anemia of chronic disease and mild chemotherapy-related myelosuppression. ANC adequate (3.2), no neutropenia. No signs or symptoms of bleeding. Will monitor serially. Iron studies ordered today to rule out iron deficiency component; will address if indicated.

5. Mild hypomagnesemia.
Will supplement with magnesium oxide 400 mg twice daily given borderline level; recheck next visit. Common with oxali regimens.

6. Goals of care.
Patient and his wife were present today. They understand the intent of therapy remains potentially curative if conversion to resectable disease is achieved, though this remains uncertain at this juncture. The response to date is encouraging and we remain cautiously optimistic. He is managing his treatment schedule well and remains engaged and motivated. His wife is very supportive. No immediate palliative care referral indicated at this time per patient preference; will reassess at future visits.

Return to clinic in 2 weeks for Cycle 6 Day 1. Patient has oncology triage number for any urgent concerns (fever, significant diarrhea, chest pain, new neurologic symptoms).

Time spent in counseling and coordination: approximately 30 minutes of 45-minute visit.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Medical Oncology
